author: @williambird884
text:
If you put your "cooling tray" on the roof then the cooled fluid will fall into the space your cooling, and the warmed fluid from the space will float back up to the cooling try. No pump required. However you will need an on going supply of water to replace the water in the tray as it evaportes. 
But if you need to keep sending water up to the roof then forget the tray and just let a mist of water coat your roof tiles. As the water evaporates it will cool the tiles. and the cool will decend to the space below.

author: @tdtrecordsmusic
text: I have always noticed that certain stuff has a propensity to keep "its temperature" and not that of the surrounding environment. Aka metal cold to the touch / water seems to always want to be near 75. I have always noticed that steel and lead(20lb bullion mind you)  in the shop "feels" colder than other materials. Maybe, i'm placebo !?! Then I check with the thermocouple and see that it appears to be negligible. Why do I think it's cold !!   " is it? "      Sure the machine might say its bla bla temperature, but it feels like yada yada. The point is NOT what the machine says, but the mechanism of which the machine responds...  in other words >> why is steel  colder than other stuffs in the metal shop !!!    If the mechanism is not understood then we are doomed to remember a gazillion discrete facts which seem like unique coincidences.

author: @Draakdarkmaster6
text:
I have a few design notes that could improve this, id like your thoughts.

Since its evaporative cooling you could have the air output on the bottom of the unit as to help the cool air flow out (as heat rises) and have a humidity output on the top to allow the evaporated water (which i assume is carrying off all the thermal energy) to escape, either into a separate water reservoir or out into the open air depending on design goals. One way i can think of to enhance its cooling effects would be to mix urea into the system and simply top it up with water whenever its dry, either manually or through a flow valve, as urea has an endothermic reaction with water it would make it almost ice cold in seconds. Once it heats up to ambient temps it would evaporate off normally and i assume the urea would be left behind allowing for another top up to start the cycle again.

author: @chuckdank3869
text: So I've read a lot of swamp cooler comparisons which I don't think are quite accurate. Swamp coolers rely on low humidity ambient air for efficiency and require a substantial fan to move it. This evaporative method relies on solar energy with the hot vapour forced into the ambient air or to a separate cooling condensing unit for recycling. Many plumbing, insulation, isolation and generation options.  Cool stuff Rob!

author: @angelacarter6593
text: That's an evaporative cooler. Here they call them swamp coolers because they often grow bacteria causing a swamp like smell. Unfortunately that doesn't work where I live because we have very high humidity and they are really best for areas where humidity levels are below 65 degrees.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
England is fairly humid on average as well.  While high humidity does slow down evaporation of course--this proposed system has a benefit over traditional swamp coolers in that the unit is not inside and adding extra humidity to the air.  But you pipe in the cooler air or water in a closed system, then it circulates back outside to the evaporation unit.  

   Throw some copper sponges and/or xylitol (which will increase the efficiency a little) into the outside container, and you won't have to worry about microbial growth.

author: @clarkmarkey1498
text: This idea reminds me of an A/C system that was used by a local aerospace manufacturer. There was a large fountain in the front lawn. the water was sprayed many meters into the air, causing some evaporation and cooling the pool. A heat exchanger in the pool was piped to an air to liquid exchanger in the building cooling and dehumidifying  the factory floor. There were associated pumps, fans ducting and whatnot. The system worked well till the advent of modern CNC machine centers which needed less variation in the environment including a very low humidity requirement. There was a hotel and a few other older facilities that used a similar system. Unfortunately, they are all gone, now.

author: @twestgard2
text: Cooling is the hardest thing to sustain off grid, so if this can be done well, it significantly advances the possibility of unplugging completely. I’m already working on the Tech Ingredients concept but this shows promise too and I’m hoping the one can increase the effects of the other.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
I was originally interested in a modified version of T.I.'s type system, but to be honest--I'm looking more at a modified, optimized version of RMS's old Adsorption Chiller video--because it is simpler, far more energy efficient, and low to no maintenance.   Looking at some of the research papers--you see references to people having designed systems based on this concept to make ice in places like Egypt.  

  Imo, the only benefit of the T.I. type system over the above, is that it also removes some humidity from the air (the adsorption chiller neither adds nor takes away humidity from the air).  But what I would do, is run a dehumidifier at night when it's cooler--these are fairly energy efficient.  Or just make my own system using a passive adsorbent (most likely 3A zeolite) and vent the warmish air outside, and then use solar heat to drive off the moisture in the adsorbent (have two containers, so you can switch them out as need be).  .

replies.author: @twestgard2
replies.text: Justin W hmm, well the devil is in the details so I’d be curious to see what you build. And maybe your goals are different from mine. For me, the goal is to disconnect entirely from the electric power grid and I suspect the dehumidifier would use too much power

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: @twestgard2   As mentioned, a passive dehumidifier is also possible.

replies.author: @twestgard2
replies.text: Justin W right on. Seems like that would bring a lot of heat back into the house or refrigerator, but I guess that’s what prototypes are for.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
@twestgard2  I'm not completely sure either way.  

  If I built such a system, I would have an insulated duct with a dedicated fan blowing the air from the insulated adsorption container outside. 

  The trick would be to get a good balance between the insulation and air porosity (to allow the adsorbition or absorption process to take place).  That would be a bit tricky. Especially since you would need insulation that wouldn't absorb significant amount of moisture itself. Fiberglass, rock wool, ceramic fiber, or Climashield Apex (interwoven polyester fibers with a  silicone coating) insulation encased in a highly air porous polypropylene or polyester fabric might work decently?   

    The ducting to outside itself doesn't need to be air porous--just needs to be insulated.  Some some Al ducting covered with insulation of some kind would work fine.

author: @gusmartin6053
text:
Doesn't the sun shining on it just serve to increase the rate of evaporation? I would imagine it would be more efficient in terms of water use being in the shade with good air flow.
If I'm not correct about that then I would be really interested in how the sun increases the temperature difference.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
Yeah, I'd be curious to know more about this as well.  Black carbon is interesting because it's both rather absorbent of Solar energy, but it's also a good black body radiator of IR. So it loses/dissipates heat almost as quick as it absorbs it.  The evaporation process in combo would only help that and speed up cooling. But at the same time, it's somewhat thermally conductive, along with the water, so it will tend to transfer some heat to the water as well.  

    It would be interesting to try a well wicking material that doesn't absorb the Solar energy much, but still helps to evaporate the water (via wicking and increased surface area).  Maybe like ceramic fiber blanket material?  Maybe also copper mesh painted white?  

  The trick would be to have a container with a very large surface area to begin with, painted all white, with the solar reflective wicking material laid across further increasing the surface area and rate of evaporation.  

   I'm thinking that Al sheeting, bent, cut, and brazed (and then painted white), would work for a suitable container for holding the water and wicking material?

text: The crazy thing about the average air conditioning solution in the UK would be the fact that most designs ignore the subsurface temperature and how perfect a sink/source it is for heat throughout the year, combine that with the superior COP of a heat pump and you have ideal system. However if you are going to make a "swampy" you can always use that to cool the air that your standard air conditioner uses for heat exchange on the external unit as reducing the differential between the two halves of the system increases its efficiency.

author: @popuptoaster
text: Would there be a way to recapture the water without increasing it's temperature so that you still got a net cooling effect? There is a You Tuber called Will Prowse who is using a conventional AC unit in his solar shed to keep the temperature of his batteries down and is managing to run it from the solar while still capturing usable amounts of energy on top. I can see that this system is much simple (cheaper!) than conventional AC but I think with water shortages becoming more common any system should consider how much water it uses as well as other concerns.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
In a semi open system like this, it would be hard.  For example, you could put a desiccant in the water, but these warm as they absorb or adsorb, which would counterbalance the evaporation cooling some.  

  To be honest, I think the old RMS video of Chiller based on a combination of refrigerant (like water and/or methanol), adsorbent (like 3A zeolite and/or activated carbon), Solar heater, and a vacuum pulled on the system would be more efficient both in cooling capacity as well as not having any loss of materials.  But it would need to be optimized with a 3rd line that includes a condenser etc, vs what he did in the video.  That and bigger containers, more material, etc.  

  It's just a bit more complicated.  But still quite easily DIY.  This will be one of my next projects.

author: @lowrads
text: Plants already do this, and for the same reason.  In fact, they are so good at wicking, some grow over 100m, a pressure differential at which water should simply boil before it reaches the canopy leaves.  Somehow though, it doesn't.  The affinity for water is likely driven by compounds such as stored malate, an intermediate in the citric acid cycle.  The draw is so strong, it can pull water across a membrane from out of a nominally saline solution.

author: @JamesWilson-pq9qp
text: It's what we call the dew point. High humidity defeats swamp coolers. Works great in Arizona with dew points below 50°f 10°c. But in New York it''s worthless with dew points above 70°f 20°c.  Interesting thing is salt will pull moisture out of the air. They call them a salt-lick. There might be a clue!

author: @AgentBenzo
text:
Anyone want a cheap/easy refrigerant cell or substrate?:
Mix crushed ACTIVATED CARBON with some type of POTASSIUM.
[[salt substitute / potassium chloride]] 
-or- 
[[Lugol's Iodine / potassium iodide]]

-- Mix.
-- Add a bit of water.
-- Seal mixture inside of a copper (metal) tube.

You have yourself a cell that will remain "refrigerator-cold" 🥶 for, at least, several months (maybe longer).
 
Possible Applications:
-- Peltier device 
-- Air conditioning 
-- Sterling Engine 
-- etc...

I can't be the first to figure out that [[carbon + potassium + water = cold]] ... and it makes me wonder why it's never discussed. 

Hope this helps somebody sweating 🥵

replies.text: Do you have any idea what is the principle behind this cooling/heat transfer effect?   Is it some kind of electron thing?

replies.author: @AgentBenzo
replies.text:
@justinw1765 -  honestly I have no idea ... I stumbled upon it by accident while making a "crystal cell" battery from a copper tube....

After numerous attempts, I was getting so fed-up from failures that I was just tossing random shyt into a pipe, as I sarcastically degraded myself with some choice names....

My final version was a carbon rod center that I fashioned from crushed activated carbon pellets that was rolled into a cylinder shape , with the help of some school glue adhesive, as a binder. 

I dropped that into a copper pipe that was filled with a solution of potassium chloride mixed with "Selsun Blue" anti-dandruff shampoo ((yeah yeah, I know... I was getting pretty desperate)).

As I was sealing the pipe, I touched this side of the tube when putting the cap on.... At first, I thought burned myself because I was not expecting any temperature differential , and it caught me off guard....
You know that feeling that you get when you touch something REALLY HOT , and it almost feels COLD for a brief brief moment before you start sizzling.... Well, that happened. 

it took me a few tries to figure out that it was somehow the potassium and carbon interacting that created such a cold reaction.

 I imagine that, on an atomic or molecular level , those particles are vibrating at a specific frequency... and when the reaction begins, that changes their vibration and somehow  causes the particles to more "coherently-fit" within a structured crystalline lattice, therefore allowing less room for the particles to freely move around now that they are structured or locked into the lattice...... 
...And when there is less movement  or vibration at the atomic level, I would assume that there would be less Heat.

I'll tell you what though. That thing does give off some strange effects in electrical circuit.

wow, this observation seems substantial.  I appreciate you sharing the details.  Definitely got my gears going. Is this phenomena something you investigated and "dialed in?"  Is my wee wee  attempt a lol and I should "get it right?"   
I am asking caz > yea I am totally on a hunt for better temperature control. To update / replace HVAC systems and to further experiments which require specific temps.  Somehow I have a hunch that there might be a way to do a "reverse thermionic emission" to produce a predictable level of thermal energy.  A "pressure motility"  irrespective of volume.      That is a fancy sounding sentence which feels like pseudoscience, but we have to start with "something!!" It is a '10 years deep concept' hinged on observations.
 Books are written and knowledge is "already" known.  To "create" we must be willing to take chances. Sometimes weird and major chances! Thus, I also hold that we must do our research on our "own coin."  To "create" we must eliminate the ideas of "Wrong vs Right / Yes vs No / Objective vs Observation / "The Goal" vs some other result which is not our goal.
 Science is real. AKA >> it has NOTHING to do with what had "set out to do."       [[ lol, sorry for the lecture/rant.  Been Covid Cooped up. wow I really went off the wagon with that one.. lol  ]]

Hey man, is this phenomena you found repeatable? Can I check it out ? What did you do again ?

author: @totherarf
text:
I use this system in my house!
My house is 110 years old with a slate roof (no felt so it leaks air but not water) and a crawl space under the floor with air bricks for airflow down there.
Basically I open my loft hatch when it gets too warm. The roof space can get very hot and when I open the trap door it allows the air to flow upwards venting through the small gaps between the slates. This creates negative air pressure in the house proper. This allows cold air from the damp crawl space to rise through the edges of the floorboards and a vent where my solid floor kitchen is a step down. 
Leaving the downstairs doors open gives a complete path for the cooler air to be dragged up and eventually exit the loft after it gets heated up there! My house is south facing but always cool in the heat of summer.
Best bit (because I am tight with money) is it is Free! ;0)

author: @geofftayler2227
text:
Rob is describing an evaporative heat exchanger..which are used all over the world in commercial HVAC systems, and the physics are thoroughly understood. The interesting bit is improving the efficiency of such devices by using a high surface area material (such as a nano particle carbon) as the surface from which the fluid is evaporated.. this is absorbing solar thermal energy to drive the evaporation, which helps offset the effect of ambient humidity..

Does it follow that a coating the evaporator coil with  a material that is both high in surface area..a good absorber of solar energy..and a good heat conductor would improve efficiency even more ?

author: @twoartistic
text: Using the sun to create a phase change is the basis for a system I designed. In the test system I built, I route that humid air up a black solar collection chimney. Then into a shaded down draft expansion chamber. Finally through a ground loop that fully condenses the water vapor back out. This uses passive energy from the sun to drive a convection flow, so no fan is needed. Plus you recollect the water and can re-use it as clean distilled water to drive the system. I have a small 1.5 watt water pump that I invented to move the water back into the evaporation chamber. The additional benefit, is it collects extra water. The humidity in the air is also condensed into extra water that can be collected in a tank. My system requires the infrastructure of underground pipe, and only works where ground temps are cold enough to condense water vapor. I envision it as a system that can be built into new construction at a minimal expense. Here in the western US, we live in a desert, so the water generation aspect is a positive aspect. I hope to build a full scale system next summer. COVID put the brakes on doing it this summer.

author: @odonald6087
text: Something you seem to consistently miss in your thinking about thermal power is the difference between temperature and heat.  Getting a large temperature differential is nice, but not going to give you a practical air conditioner, for that you need both a temperature difference and actual movement of heat.  That means you need to be thinking about the available heat movement power, which is going to be essentially 0 for something encased in foam and relying on diffusion and capillary action.   (And for a baseline of efficiency, my heat pump has a measured COP of about 4, which means it's giving me 4W of cooling for each watt of electricity I'm spending.  My solar panels are about 25% efficient, which means that my heat pump is close to 100% efficient at turning sun watts into cold watts.)

replies.author: @odonald6087
replies.text: @SystemsPlanet It won't conduct enough to be worthwhile, the two methods that work in practice are fluid transfer (e.g. water in a pipe) and phase change (e.g. propane evaporating inside a pipe).

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
Yeah, he mentions this, which is why he says you can run pipes from outside to inside to transfer the cooling--that way you're not adding extra humidity to the inside air.    

    As far as humidity goes, you could theoretically build an insulated, but air porous container to hold desiccants (3A Zeolite might be one of the most efficient?), and then blow the warmed up air outside?   These generate some heat as they are adsorbing the humidity from the air, so if you want to not heat up the inside--you can blow that air through an insulated air duct.  

    Ideally, you would have two containers going, so that as one saturates with water, you can switch it out, and then use a Solar cooker/heating system, to regenerate (drive off the water) from the desiccant.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
@mekuranda   No, I know these are not as efficient in high humidity--same concept behind humans not cooling down well in high humidity.  

  But a couple points.  England actually has fairly high humidity levels on average--in July and August--average ranges from 60 to 90%. 

    Unless the air is fully/completely saturated i.e. at or near 100&, you will get some evaporation--especially since you are forcing/speeding up the process some with Solar heat.  

    Since I live in the northern'ish part of the south east of the US---I would go with the adsorption cooler concept most likely, because while the evap cooler would work some most of the time, the adsorption cooler would be more efficient I think.  RMS has an old video on this, look up adsorption chiller.  

     Just make it optimized for efficiency. (There is plenty of research out there on these type systems, which typically either use water or methanol as the refrigerant, and a moderate vacuum to lower the boiling temps). 

   I think you may be missing that htmagic seemed to be referring to traditional forms of evap coolers, which typically add humidity directly into the inside air, which would be a big no no in our kind of climates (and also in England).  That is what I was referring to.  Just pointing out that at least RMS's system here would not be adding extra humidity to the inside air.

author: @donaldhanson2908
text:
We ran an evaporative cooler for forty five years. 
They work wonderfully well here in the south of California, until we get tropical air coming up loaded with warm water. (Which we have right now)
Very simple system, a squirrel cage blower in a box, with excelsior mats on the sides and a low power water pump in a self leveling reservoir, the pump ran a trickle of water to the top of the mats which is then recovered on the reservoir.
Even running the fan at high speed used only a few hundred watts. 
As I say, the fail point was always atmospheric moisture.

author: @NeoShameMan
text:
Yep an African inventor is working on something similar, basically water stole heat to evaporate, the increased interface of the foam help that. But when i see temperatures differential, that big, i see Stirling engine, could the water pump be self sustained by it?

Also that's wasting water, is there a way to make a close circuit that condensate back water? What about using vaccum to help the process?

I have  been thinking about how to  mix that with desalination and salt production.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
Look up RMS's old video on adsorbtion chiller.  I find that more promising than this to be honest.  In that old video--he just threw something together quick to show proof of concept--but if it's optimized for efficiency, it would work a lot better.  Research groups have made units using this concept which makes large, constant amounts of ice in places like Egypt (course, these kinds of units tend to be rather large--and they have a lot of consistent, intense sun there).  

  But yes, it takes advantage of moderate vacuum to decrease the boiling temperatures of the refrigerant (water and/or methanol), along with passive adsorbents (3A zeolite for water, and activated carbon for methanol), and solar heat/concentrator to separate the refrigerant molecules from the adsorbent.  

   To optimize the system, you basically want to 1. use higher volume and mass of materials, 2. add a third line with a condenser to help the phase change from gas back to liquid.  Plus there are minor tweaks to slightly up efficiency, such as wrap copper tubes  filled with graphite and some water, around inside metal, cooling unit. Maybe throw some copper sponges inside there as well.

  Insulate outside glass with adsorbent should have dark and/or thermally conductive materials mixed in with the adsorbent (course, activated carbon with methanol already has an advantage in that area), such as graphite.  And be well insulated.  

   The problem is, these only run energy free during the day.  

  However, one could use radiative cooling to continue the cooling process into the night.   For example, build a large funnerl/inverted Solar cooker type design.  Put water in an IR passing container that is insulated and run insulated pipes from outside to inside.  

   As the funnel helps the water/container to lose/redirect the IR up into space, the temperature of the materials cools down.  Protect it from conductive and convective heat gain (closed container that is well insulated), and then circulate that inside, and you have a nighttime cooling mechanism.  

     If you're clever enough, you might be able to combine the two systems, though, to be honest, it would easier to have them separate, because you will need an active, electricially driven pump for the night time radiative cooling most likely?

author: @Cooliemasteroz
